visual_checks: Scan the page for abbreviations, acronyms, and initialisms
Check if the first occurrence of each abbreviation is expanded (e.g., 'World Wide Web Consortium (W3C)')
Look for abbr elements with title attributes that provide the expanded form
Check if a glossary or abbreviation list is provided and linked from the page
Look for abbreviations that are used without ever being expanded or defined
Identify abbreviations that could have multiple meanings in different contexts
pass_conditions: Every abbreviation is expanded on first use in the content (e.g., 'HyperText Markup Language (HTML)')
The abbr element with a title attribute is used to provide the expanded form of abbreviations
A glossary or abbreviation list is available and linked that covers all abbreviations on the page
Abbreviations with multiple possible meanings are clarified in context
At least one mechanism is provided to identify the expanded form of every abbreviation
fail_conditions: Abbreviations are used without ever providing their expanded form
The expanded form is provided only deep within the site and not accessible from the current page
abbr elements are used but lack title attributes with the expansion
Common abbreviations in the domain are assumed to be universally known without expansion
No mechanism exists to determine what an abbreviation stands for
na_conditions: Page contains no abbreviations, acronyms, or initialisms
ne_conditions: AI response could not be parsed
Screenshots could not be captured for visual verification
Abbreviations could not be reliably identified in the page content
common_mistakes: Expanding an abbreviation only on the homepage but not on subpages where it also appears
Using abbr elements without title attributes, providing no actual expansion
Assuming common abbreviations like 'FAQ' or 'PDF' need no expansion for all audiences
Providing expansions in a glossary that is not linked or discoverable from the current page
Expanding abbreviations in body text but not in navigation, headings, or form labels

examples.pass: The page expands 'WCAG' as 'Web Content Accessibility Guidelines (WCAG)' on first use, and all subsequent instances use the abbr element with title='Web Content Accessibility Guidelines'
examples.fail: The page uses 'ARIA', 'WCAG', 'AT', and 'DOM' repeatedly without ever expanding them or providing a linked glossary — users unfamiliar with these abbreviations cannot determine their meaning
examples.partial: Most abbreviations are expanded on first use, but three abbreviations introduced in a sidebar widget are never expanded and no abbr elements are used for them

plain_meaning: Abbreviations and acronyms used in the content must have some mechanism available that reveals their expanded form or intended meaning. This can be the first-occurrence expansion, an abbr element with a title, a glossary, or any other available mechanism.
scope_applies_to: Acronyms and initialisms used in the content
Shortened forms of organization or program names
Domain-specific abbreviations in technical or academic content
First and subsequent occurrences of abbreviations in running text
scope_does_not_apply_to: Abbreviations whose meaning is universally known in the content's context
Units of measurement that are standard (for example kg, cm)
Proper names that are formally abbreviated (for example a company whose legal name is the abbreviation)
Words with internal capitalization that are not abbreviations
Pronunciation of the abbreviation (that is SC 3.1.6)
borderline_calibration: Any one mechanism is enough: abbr title, inline expansion at first use, or a glossary.
Do not require every subsequent occurrence to be re-expanded once a mechanism is available.
PASS when the expansion is present in the same paragraph or nearby.
FAIL only when abbreviations are genuinely used AND no expansion mechanism of any kind is offered.
